_README: AP CSP QUIZ — QUESTION BANK (source of truth)
This file holds every question used by the AP CSP Quiz app.
JSON has no native comment syntax, so we use keys starting with
an underscore ('_README', '_doc', '_note', etc.) for documentation.
These keys are IGNORED by the app at runtime — they're for humans
and for Claude when extending the bank.
HOW TO EXTEND THIS FILE:
1) Open this file with Claude.ai and say something like:
'Add 10 more multiple_choice questions to the procedures
topic, following the schema in _typeGuide.'
2) Claude reads _typeGuide and the existing questions to learn
the format, then appends new ones to the 'questions' array.
3) When you're ready to update the app, paste the new
'questions' array into the QUESTIONS constant at the top
of app.jsx (or ask Claude to do it).
QUESTION TYPES (see _typeGuide below for full schemas):
multiple_choice      — classic A/B/C/D
trace                — animated step-through with live variables
binary               — toggle 8 bits to match a target decimal
bug_hunt             — click the buggy line, then identify the bug
boolean              — rapid TRUE / FALSE evaluation
build_loop           — reorder scrambled pseudocode blocks
match_pairs          — pair items between two columns (1:1)
fill_in_blank        — code with {{N}} blanks filled by dropdowns
click_to_locate_bug  — tap the buggy line (no follow-up MCQ)
order_steps          — drag plain-text items into the right order
TOPICS (used for grouping and per-topic scoring):
procedures, iteration, data, efficiency, debugging,
boolean, binary, media, internet, security, impact
AP PSEUDOCODE CONVENTIONS USED THROUGHOUT:
* Lists are 1-INDEXED:  aList[1] is the FIRST element
* <- (or ←) means assignment;  =  means equality test
* MOD is modulo;  LENGTH(list)  gives list size
* APPEND(list, value), INSERT(list, i, value), REMOVE(list, i)
* RANDOM(a, b) returns a random integer from a to b inclusive
* Procedures: PROCEDURE name(params) { ... RETURN(value) }
* Loops: REPEAT n TIMES { },  REPEAT UNTIL (cond) { },
FOR EACH item IN list { }

_meta._doc_topics: Each topic maps to its full name, short label, and study reference. The app reads these for rendering. Don't change keys without updating app.jsx.

media.name: Pixels & Compression
media.study: Barron's Ch 3 · Hare: Pixels & Compressing Data

impact.name: Impact of Computing
impact.study: Barron's Ch 6 · Hare: Impact of Computing

_typeGuide._doc: Schema reference for every question type. When adding questions, follow the schema exactly. Required fields are marked. Optional fields can be omitted.

multiple_choice._when_to_use: Default workhorse. Use for any conceptual question or short code-trace question with one correct answer out of four.

_schema.topic: string (REQUIRED, one of the topic keys in _meta.topics)
_schema.question: string (REQUIRED, the question prompt; may include \n for line breaks)
_schema.code: string (OPTIONAL, rendered as a separate code block under the question)

trace._when_to_use: Use when the value of intermediate variables matters for understanding. Student steps through line-by-line, watches variables update, then answers a final-state question. Best for nested loops, swaps, accumulators.

_schema.title: string (REQUIRED, short label e.g. 'Trace the swap')
_schema.code: array of strings (REQUIRED, one per line; preserve indentation with leading spaces)
_schema.steps: array of step objects (REQUIRED, in execution order). Each step is { 'line': N, 'vars': { name: value, ... } } where 'line' is 0-indexed and 'vars' is the COMPLETE variable state at that moment (including unchanged vars). Lists/arrays should be passed as their string form, e.g. '[3, 5]'.
_schema.question: string (REQUIRED, asked after stepping through)

_authoring_tips: Keep traces under 15 steps — students lose focus past that.
Render lists/arrays as strings like '[3, 5]' to keep JSON simple.
Show variables on the step they FIRST appear, then keep showing them every subsequent step.
For procedure return values, use synthetic var names like 'f(4)' to expose the return.

binary._when_to_use: ONLY for the binary topic. Student sees 8 toggleable bit-switches with place values (128,64,32,16,8,4,2,1) and flips them to match a target. Live decimal counter shows current value as they toggle.

_schema.explanation: string (REQUIRED, shown after match; show breakdown like '173 = 128+32+8+4+1 = 10101101')

_authoring_tips: Mix easy targets (under 32, 1-2 bits) with hard ones (over 100, multiple bits).
Avoid 0 and 255 (too trivial / too obvious).
Vary bit patterns — don't make them all rounded numbers.

bug_hunt._when_to_use: Best for debugging topic. Student first clicks the buggy line in a code snippet, then identifies what KIND of bug it is from 4 options. Awards extra credit for getting both right.

_schema.code: array of strings (REQUIRED, one per line; use leading spaces for indentation)
_schema.options: array of EXACTLY 4 strings (REQUIRED, descriptions of the bug type)
_schema.answer: number 0–3 (REQUIRED, index of correct bug-type description)

_authoring_tips: Mix bug types: infinite loops, off-by-one, dead code, missing return, wrong operator, wrong scope.
Make wrong options PLAUSIBLE — vague distractors aren't educational.
Keep code 5-8 lines so the student can scan quickly.

_example.explanation: i goes 0,3,6,9,12... never hits exactly 10. Use i ≥ 10 instead.

boolean._when_to_use: Rapid-fire TRUE/FALSE evaluation. Student sees an expression with literal true/false values and operators (AND, OR, NOT) and picks one of two huge buttons. Best for the boolean topic.

_schema.expression: string (REQUIRED, the expression e.g. 'NOT(true AND false)')

_authoring_tips: Keep expressions visually short (under ~30 chars).
Mix difficulties: simple → nested → with multiple operators.
Vary parens to test order-of-operations.
Don't use VARIABLE names — only literal true/false. (For variable-based reasoning, use multiple_choice.)

_example.explanation: Inside first: true AND false = false. NOT(false) = true.

build_loop._when_to_use: Student is given pseudocode blocks scrambled and must rearrange them. Great for teaching the SHAPE of common patterns (sum, max, filter, count). Best for iteration and procedures topics.

_schema.title: string (REQUIRED, e.g. 'Sum a list')
_schema.description: string (REQUIRED, what the assembled program should do)
_schema.blocks: array of strings (REQUIRED, the LINES OF CODE in their CORRECT ORDER. The app shuffles them on display. Preserve indentation with leading spaces.)

_authoring_tips: 5–8 blocks is the sweet spot.
Closing braces } get their own line — students should learn placement.
Each block should be a logically complete line.
If two orderings would both work, that's a flaw — make sure there's exactly one correct order.

_example.title: Sum a list
_example.description: Order these blocks to correctly sum the values in nums.
_example.explanation: Standard accumulator pattern: initialize, loop and update, then display.

match_pairs._when_to_use: Two parallel concepts that have a 1:1 mapping. Great for: term ↔ definition, algorithm ↔ Big-O, file extension ↔ format kind. Tap a left item, tap a right item to pair them.

_schema.prompt: string (REQUIRED, what the student should match)
_schema.right: array of strings (REQUIRED, MUST be same length as left). Order is independent of left — the matching is via 'answer'.
_schema.answer: array of indices into 'right' (REQUIRED, same length as left). answer[i] is the right-index that pairs with left[i]. Must be a one-to-one mapping (no repeated indices).

_authoring_tips: 3–4 pairs is the sweet spot. 5 is the max for usability.
Make every wrong-pair plausible to a half-prepared student — avoid filler.
Order the right-hand side so the correct mapping is NOT just [0,1,2,...] — vary it.
Keep each item under ~30 chars so cells don't wrap awkwardly on phones.

_example.prompt: Match each algorithm to its worst-case time complexity.
_example.explanation: Linear scan = O(n). Binary halves = O(log n). Bubble compares every pair = O(n²). Hash maps to a bucket directly = O(1).

fill_in_blank._when_to_use: Code with one or more {{N}} placeholders, each filled by a small dropdown. Tests precise syntax recall and operator/function selection. Best when the student should already know the SHAPE and just needs to drop in the right pieces.

_schema.question: string (REQUIRED, what the completed code should do)
_schema.code: array of strings (REQUIRED, one per line). Use {{0}}, {{1}}, ... wherever a blank goes. Preserve indentation with leading spaces.
_schema.blanks: array of objects (REQUIRED, in numerical order). Each blank: { 'options': ['a','b','c','d'] (REQUIRED, exactly 4), 'answer': 0–3 (REQUIRED) }.

_authoring_tips: 1–3 blanks per question. More than 3 becomes a slot-fill puzzle, not a learning tool.
Each blank's 4 options should be plausible: same TYPE (operators with operators, values with values).
Avoid blanks at the START of a line — students lose the visual anchor.
Don't use {{N}} inside string literals; the renderer will turn it into a select.

_example.question: Complete the code so it sums the values in nums.
_example.explanation: Initialize accumulator to 0, then add each value into it.

click_to_locate_bug._when_to_use: Show buggy code and have the student tap the offending line. Simpler cousin of bug_hunt — no follow-up MCQ for bug TYPE. Use when the teaching value is 'spot the bug', not 'name its category'.

_schema.question: string (OPTIONAL, override the default 'Tap the line containing the bug.' prompt)
_schema.code: array of strings (REQUIRED, one per line; use leading spaces for indentation)
_schema.explanation: string (REQUIRED, point at the bug AND say what would fix it)

_authoring_tips: 5–10 lines of code. Too few and there's no spotting; too many and it's a reading test.
Make sure NO OTHER LINE could plausibly be 'the bug' — only one true defect.
Save bug_hunt for cases where the bug TYPE is the more interesting question.

_example.explanation: Returning true on the FIRST positive is wrong — we should only return true after confirming ALL are positive. The fix: return false immediately when we find a non-positive, and return true after the loop.

order_steps._when_to_use: Reorder a list of items into the correct sequence. Generalization of build_loop for non-code content: ordering steps of an algorithm in plain English, sorting Big-O complexities by speed, putting historical events in order. Use build_loop for actual code-block ordering.

_schema.title: string (REQUIRED, short label)
_schema.description: string (REQUIRED, what 'in order' means here — fastest first? alphabetical? chronological?)
_schema.items: array of strings (REQUIRED, 3–6 items, in their CORRECT ORDER. The app shuffles for display.)

_authoring_tips: 3–5 items. 6+ feels like work, not learning.
Make sure there's EXACTLY ONE correct order — ties are a flaw.
If items are code, prefer build_loop instead — its mono styling fits better.

_example.title: Sort by speed (fastest first)
_example.description: Order these time complexities from fastest to slowest for very large n.
_example.explanation: Constant beats logarithmic beats linear beats linearithmic beats quadratic. As n grows, the gap between any two is enormous.

_topicWeighting_doc: The current bank intentionally over-weights topics where the student showed weakness in his Minesweeper code (procedures, iteration, data, efficiency, debugging). Approximate distribution: procedures ~15, iteration ~15, data ~12, efficiency ~12, debugging ~12, boolean ~12, binary ~12 (incl. bit-flip), media ~8, internet ~10, security ~8, impact ~8. Adjust as you add questions.

questions.question: What is returned by max(7, 3)?
questions.explanation: 7 > 3 is true, so the IF branch runs and returns a, which is 7.

questions.question: What is the main reason to use procedures?
options: They let you reuse code and update logic in one place
They store data more compactly in memory
questions.explanation: Procedural abstraction: wrap reusable logic in a named procedure. Fix once, fixed everywhere.

questions.question: Which best describes a parameter?
options: A variable that holds a value passed in by the caller
questions.explanation: In PROCEDURE greet(name), 'name' is a parameter. greet("Sam") passes "Sam" into name.

questions.question: What does mystery(mystery(3)) return?
questions.code: PROCEDURE mystery(x) { RETURN(x * 2 + 1) }
questions.explanation: Inner first: mystery(3) = 3*2+1 = 7. Then mystery(7) = 7*2+1 = 15.

questions.question: A procedure always draws a red square at (100, 100). To make it more reusable:
options: Add parameters for position and color
Move it to a different file
questions.explanation: Parameters generalize a procedure. drawSquare(x, y, color) draws any square anywhere.

questions.question: Why is RETURN useful?
options: So the procedure can be called more than once
So the result can be used in further computation
questions.explanation: Return values let you plug a procedure into expressions: total ← price + tax(price).

questions.question: What is the value of sum?
questions.code:
PROCEDURE addThree(a, b, c) { RETURN(a + b + c) }
sum ← addThree(2, addThree(1, 1, 1), 4)
questions.explanation: Inner addThree(1,1,1) = 3. Outer = addThree(2, 3, 4) = 9.

questions.question: Which design for an average procedure is best?
options: PROCEDURE average(nums) — takes list, returns average
No params, displays answer, returns nothing
questions.explanation: Inputs as parameters, output as return value, no hidden side effects. Cleanest design.

questions.question: What is a 'side effect' of a procedure?
options: Anything besides returning a value (modifying a global, drawing, displaying)
questions.explanation: Side effects = observable changes outside the return value. Pure-return procedures are easiest to test.

questions.question: What does g(4) return?
questions.code:
PROCEDURE f(x) { RETURN(x + 1) }
PROCEDURE g(x) { RETURN(f(x) * 2) }
questions.explanation: f(4) = 5. Then 5 * 2 = 10. Procedures can call other procedures.

questions.question: Which body for isEven(n) is correct?
questions.explanation: n MOD 2 gives the remainder. If 0, n is even. Option B returns true for ODD numbers (wrong polarity); option C returns 0 or 1 instead of true/false.

questions.question: Beyond reuse, why is a well-named procedure like isPrime(n) easier to TEST than the same logic inlined inside main?
options: The procedure runs in a separate program
You can call it directly with chosen inputs (5, 7, 9, 100) and check the return, without running the rest of main
The compiler tests it for you
questions.explanation: Procedures isolate logic so you can test it on its own — call with edge cases, check the return. Inlined code can only be exercised by running the whole program around it.

questions.question: What does h(h(10, 4), 3) return?
questions.code: PROCEDURE h(a, b) { RETURN(a - b) }
questions.explanation: Inner h(10,4) = 6. Outer h(6,3) = 3.

questions.question: What's wrong with this procedure?
options: It computes a value but never RETURNs it
questions.explanation: y is calculated then discarded. Without RETURN(y), the caller gets nothing.

questions.title: Build a procedure
questions.description: Order these blocks to define a procedure 'square' that returns x², then call it on 5.
questions.explanation: Define first, then call. RETURN sends the value back to the caller.

questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.code:
nums ← [4, 7, 2, 9, 5]
total ← 0
FOR EACH n IN nums { total ← total + n }
DISPLAY(total)
questions.explanation: FOR EACH visits every item. 4+7+2+9+5 = 27.

questions.question: AP pseudocode lists are 1-indexed. Value of aList[3]?
questions.explanation: AP lists start at 1, NOT 0. aList[1]=10, aList[2]=20, aList[3]=30.

questions.question: Value of count?
questions.explanation: Three of five words are 'cat'.

questions.question: What displays?
questions.explanation: Loops 4 times. i starts at 1, displayed before each increment: 1, 2, 3, 4.

questions.title: Trace the accumulator
questions.question: What value is displayed?
questions.explanation: FOR EACH adds 3, then 5, then 7 → 15.

questions.title: Trace the filter
questions.question: What value is displayed?
questions.explanation: Only values > 2 are appended: 3, 4, 5. LENGTH = 3.

questions.title: Trace nested loops
questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.explanation: Inner loop adds (10+20)=30 each outer iteration. Two outer iterations → 60.

questions.question: After this code, what is nums?
questions.code:
nums ← [3, 6, 9]
APPEND(nums, 12)
INSERT(nums, 1, 0)
questions.explanation: APPEND adds 12 to end. INSERT(list, 1, 0) puts 0 at position 1.

questions.question: To check 4 squares around (x,y), instead of 4 nearly-identical IFs:
options: Store offsets [-1,0],[1,0],[0,-1],[0,1] and use FOR EACH
questions.explanation: Copy-paste with one variable change = use a list and a loop. Many lines → few.

questions.question: What does this code do?
questions.explanation: Standard 'maximum' pattern. Result: 9.

questions.title: Find the max
questions.description: Order these blocks to find the largest value in nums.
questions.explanation: Initialize with first element, scan and update on bigger values.

questions.title: Filter evens
questions.description: Order these blocks to build a new list with only even values from nums.
questions.explanation: Filter pattern: empty result, scan, append when condition holds.

questions.title: Count matches
questions.description: Order these blocks to count how many items in nums equal target.
questions.explanation: Counter pattern: zero out, scan, increment on match.

questions.question: What does this display?
questions.explanation: 10, 7, 4, 1. Next would be -2, but loop checks i ≤ 0 first → stops.

questions.question: What is sum?
questions.explanation: Only 30 and 40 exceed 25. sum = 30 + 40 = 70.

questions.question: What is data abstraction?
options: Organizing data so programs work with concepts (cell, student) instead of many primitives
questions.explanation: Data abstraction = choose a structure matching the problem. A grid problem deserves a 2D list.

questions.question: What is grid[2][3]?
Track each student's name, grade, and GPA. Best AP-style abstraction:
options: One string per student combining all three
questions.explanation: Parallel lists indexed together. Or list of sublists. Both appear on the exam.

questions.question: After grid[2][2] ← 5, grid is:
questions.explanation: Row 2, col 2 is the center of the 3×3 grid (1-indexed).

questions.question: Best data structure for tic-tac-toe board?
options: A 2D list (list of lists) representing rows and columns
questions.explanation: 2D list mirrors the grid directly. board[row][col] reads naturally.

questions.question: You're tracking 30 students' attendance for 180 school days (present/absent). Best AP-style data abstraction?
options: A 2D list with 30 rows × 180 columns: attendance[student][day]
questions.explanation: A 2D list mirrors the natural structure. Per-student totals = sum a row; per-day totals = sum a column. Separate variables can't be looped over.

questions.question: Which expression accesses the LAST grade in 1-indexed list 'grades'?
questions.explanation: AP lists are 1-indexed → last is at LENGTH(grades). In Python it'd be LENGTH-1.

questions.question: Why might parallel lists (names and ages) be a poor design?
options: They can become out of sync — adding a name without an age breaks pairing
questions.explanation: Parallel lists require discipline: every add/remove updates BOTH lists.

questions.question: Storing temperatures every hour for a week. Reasonable abstraction?
options: Either flat list or 2D list works
questions.explanation: Multiple good abstractions. 2D makes 'Monday avg' easy; flat makes 'week avg' easy.

questions.question: What is aList?
questions.code:
aList ← [1, 2, 3]
APPEND(aList, 4)
APPEND(aList, aList[1])
questions.explanation: Append 4. Then append aList[1] which is 1.

questions.question: grid[1][1] is:
questions.explanation: grid[1] = ["X","O","X"], then [1] gives "X". 1-indexed top-left.

questions.question: Total after this code?
questions.explanation: Sum every cell: 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21.

questions.question: Best describes linear search?
options: Needs sorted; checks middle then halves
Checks each element in order until target found or end reached
questions.explanation: Walks through one item at a time. Works on any list.

questions.question: REQUIRED for binary search to work:
questions.explanation: Binary search needs sorted data to compare to the middle.

questions.question: Worst-case comparisons for linear search on 1,000,000 items?
questions.explanation: Worst case: target at the end (or absent) — check all 1,000,000 items. About 500,000 would be the AVERAGE case (n/2), not worst.

questions.question: Worst-case comparisons for binary search on 1,000,000 SORTED items?
questions.explanation: Each step halves. log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20.

questions.question: 'Reasonable time' grows like:
options: A polynomial of n (n, n², n³, ...)
questions.explanation: Reasonable = polynomial. Unreasonable = exponential or factorial.

questions.question: Cracking a password by trying every possibility — each extra character roughly multiplies time. This is:
questions.explanation: Brute-forcing grows exponentially. That's why long passwords are secure.

questions.question: What is a heuristic?
options: An algorithm that always finds the optimal solution
A 'good enough' approach when optimal would take too long
questions.explanation: Heuristics trade optimality for speed. GPS picking a fast-ish route in seconds.

questions.question: A: linear time (n steps). B: quadratic (n² steps). For very large n:
questions.explanation: For n=1M: A = 1M steps, B = 1 trillion steps. Linear beats quadratic.

questions.question: A problem is 'undecidable' if:
options: No algorithm can solve it for all inputs in finite time
questions.explanation: Undecidable = no algorithm exists for all inputs. Famous example: the Halting Problem.

questions.question: Find a name in an UNSORTED list of 100. Which approach?
questions.explanation: Binary needs sorted data. Linear is correct here.

questions.question: An algorithm runs in time proportional to its input size. This is:
questions.explanation: Linear time = steps proportional to n. Doubling input ~= doubling time.

questions.question: Why is sorting before binary search worth it for repeated searches?
options: Pay sort cost once, then enjoy log(n) lookups forever
questions.explanation: Pay sort cost once, then enjoy fast lookups forever.

questions.question: What is iterative development?
options: Writing the whole program at once
questions.explanation: Build → test → fix → build the next piece. Required for the Create Performance Task.

questions.question: Program crashes on negative input. Best first step:
options: Identify exactly which line crashes and what triggers it
questions.explanation: Reproduce and isolate. Only then can you fix.
i goes 0,3,6,9,12... never hits exactly 10. Use i ≥ 10 instead.

options: y is computed but never RETURNed — result is empty
questions.explanation: double() calculates y but never returns it. Add RETURN(y).

options: Dead code — line 0's value is overwritten on line 2 before being used
questions.explanation: Line 0 picks a random mine, but line 2 picks a new one before the first is used.

options: Should be sum ← sum + n (currently overwrites instead of accumulating)
questions.explanation: Line 2 keeps overwriting sum. Final value is just the LAST element.

options: 'not found' prints for every non-matching item — should print once at the end
questions.explanation: If list has 10 items and target is 3rd, you'll see 9 'not found' lines. Move ELSE OUTSIDE the loop.

options: RETURN values are swapped — should return a when a > b, not b
questions.explanation: When a > b, the bigger value is a. Currently returns the smaller.

questions.question: A 'boundary' or 'edge' case is:
options: Input at extreme limits (empty, 0, very large, negative)
questions.explanation: Boundary cases hide bugs. Always test empty, single-element, very large, zero, negative.

questions.question: What is 'dead code'?
options: Code that can never be reached, or whose result is overwritten before use
questions.explanation: Like x ← 5 immediately followed by x ← 10.

questions.question: Why test after each small change?
options: When a bug appears, you know it came from the most recent change
questions.explanation: Test small, find bugs fast.

questions.question: Program works on small lists but crashes on lists with 1 million items. Likely:
options: A performance/efficiency problem (too slow or out of memory)
questions.explanation: Bugs that scale with input size are usually efficiency bugs.
AND is true ONLY when both operands are true.
OR is true if AT LEAST ONE operand is true.
NOT flips the value.
Inside first: true AND false = false. NOT(false) = true.
Inside first: true OR false = true. NOT(true) = false.
Left: false. Right: true. false OR true = true.
Double negative cancels.

questions.question: If a = 5 and b = 10, what is (a > 3) AND (b < 8)?
questions.explanation: (5 > 3) = true. (10 < 8) = false. true AND false = false.

questions.question: Equivalent to NOT(x > 5)?
questions.explanation: Opposite of 'greater than 5' = 'less than OR EQUAL to 5'.

questions.question: Equivalent to NOT(a AND b)? (De Morgan's Law)
questions.explanation: De Morgan: NOT(a AND b) = (NOT a) OR (NOT b).

questions.question: Equivalent to NOT(a OR b)?
questions.explanation: De Morgan part 2. When NOT moves in, AND/OR swap.

questions.question: Users may enter if age ≥ 18 OR has parental permission. Correct expression:
questions.explanation: 'A or B' translates to OR.

questions.question: Decimal value of binary 1011?
Distinct values with 4 bits?
Bits in a byte?
questions.explanation: A byte = 8 bits.

questions.question: Why do computers use binary?
questions.explanation: Hardware reliability. Two states are easy to distinguish.

questions.question: What is overflow error?
options: Number too large to represent in available bits
questions.explanation: 8-bit max = 255. Try to store 256 → wrap to 0 or error.

questions.question: RGB uses 8 bits each for R, G, B. Distinct colors?
questions.explanation: 256 × 256 × 256 ≈ 16.7 million. 'True color.'

questions.question: Hexadecimal (base 16) uses how many distinct symbols?
questions.explanation: 0–9 plus A–F. Two hex digits = one byte.

questions.question: A digital image is fundamentally:
options: A grid of pixels, each storing a color
questions.explanation: Every digital image is a 2D array of pixels.

questions.question: Lossy compression:
options: Discards some info to make files smaller
questions.explanation: JPEG drops detail your eye won't notice.

questions.question: Lossless compression:
questions.explanation: ZIP, PNG, FLAC. Decompressed = bit-for-bit identical.

questions.question: Which is a LOSSLESS image format?
questions.explanation: PNG is lossless. JPEG is lossy.

questions.question: Which is a LOSSY audio format?
questions.explanation: MP3 is lossy. WAV is uncompressed (effectively lossless).

questions.question: Doubling the sampling rate of an audio recording (e.g., 22 kHz → 44 kHz) generally:
options: Captures more high-frequency detail but roughly doubles the file size
Has no effect on the file
Converts the audio from lossy to lossless
questions.explanation: More samples per second = more detail captured AND more bytes stored. CDs sample at 44.1 kHz; phone calls at ~8 kHz to save bandwidth.

questions.question: An uncompressed 100×100-pixel image with 24 bits of color per pixel takes about how much storage?
questions.explanation: 100 × 100 = 10,000 pixels, each 24 bits = 240,000 bits = 30,000 bytes ≈ 30 KB. Compression (e.g., PNG, JPEG) can shrink this dramatically.

questions.question: Sampling an analog signal converts it digital by:
options: Recording its value at regular intervals
questions.explanation: Sampling bridges analog (continuous) to digital (discrete). CD samples 44,100/sec.

questions.question: What is an IP address?
options: A unique identifier for a device on a network
questions.explanation: Every device needs a unique IP. Like 192.168.1.5.

questions.question: Internet data is broken into smaller chunks called:
questions.explanation: Packets allow many paths, fault recovery, and shared capacity.

questions.question: What does DNS do?
options: Translates domain names (example.com) into IP addresses
questions.explanation: DNS = the Internet's phonebook.

questions.question: The Internet is 'fault tolerant' because:
options: If one path fails, data takes a different route
questions.explanation: Multiple redundant paths mean a single failure can't take down the whole Internet.

questions.question: Internet vs World Wide Web:
options: Internet = global network of devices; Web = one service (websites via HTTP)
questions.explanation: Internet = infrastructure. Web = one app on top of it.

questions.question: A 'protocol' in networking is:
options: Agreed-upon rules for how devices communicate
questions.explanation: Like a language — both sides must agree on format.

questions.question: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP doesn't?
questions.explanation: S = Secure. HTTPS encrypts traffic.

questions.question: 'Bandwidth' is:
options: Data that can be transmitted per unit time
questions.explanation: Measured in bps, Mbps, Gbps.

questions.question: 'Redundancy' in network design means:
options: Sending packets many times to slow others
Multiple paths between devices, so one path failing doesn't break the network
questions.explanation: Redundancy = fault tolerance enabler.

questions.question: Packet from CA to NY passes through how many routers?
options: Many — a dozen+; route may differ packet to packet
questions.explanation: Packets hop router to router. Different packets in same conversation can take different paths.

questions.question: In SYMMETRIC encryption:
options: Same secret key for encrypt and decrypt
questions.explanation: Same key both sides. Fast, but you must share it securely first.

questions.question: In PUBLIC-KEY (asymmetric) encryption:
options: Each person has a public key (shared) and private key (secret); messages encrypted with public can only be decrypted with matching private
questions.explanation: Anyone can encrypt TO me using my public key. Only I (private) can read.

questions.question: An HTTPS lock icon in your browser tells you:
options: The website is trustworthy and safe to share data with
The connection is encrypted AND the certificate matches the domain — but the site itself could still be a scam
All data on the site is private even from the website owner
questions.explanation: HTTPS guarantees the channel is encrypted and you're talking to the domain you typed. It does NOT vouch for the site's honesty — phishing sites use HTTPS too.

questions.question: What is phishing?
options: Tricking someone into revealing info by pretending to be trustworthy
questions.explanation: Verify suspicious requests through a separate channel.

questions.question: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) improves security by:
questions.explanation: Even if password is stolen, attacker still needs your phone or fingerprint.

questions.question: What is malware?
options: Software designed to harm, exploit, or gain unauthorized access
questions.explanation: Umbrella term: viruses, worms, ransomware, spyware, trojans.

questions.question: You use a long, random, unique password for every site. Why does multi-factor authentication still help?
options: Long passwords stop being secure after one year
If a site's password database leaks, the attacker still needs your second factor (phone, key) to log in
MFA encrypts your password during transmission
questions.explanation: Database breaches expose even strong passwords. The second factor — something you HAVE (phone, hardware key) or ARE (fingerprint) — blocks the attacker from completing the login.

questions.question: Main reason to keep software updated:
options: Updates patch security vulnerabilities attackers exploit
questions.explanation: New vulnerabilities are found constantly. Updates fix them.

questions.question: 'Digital divide' refers to:
options: Gap between those with and without access to modern computing tech
questions.explanation: Affects job access, education, healthcare. Income, geography, age, national lines.

questions.question: Algorithmic bias occurs when:
options: Biased training data or design causes systematically unfair results for certain groups
questions.explanation: Facial recognition trained on one demographic does worse on others.

questions.question: Creative Commons is:
options: Standardized licenses for sharing/reuse of creative work
questions.explanation: CC BY, CC BY-SA, CC BY-NC. Clear rules without giving up copyright.

questions.question: Personally Identifiable Information (PII) includes:
options: Info that can identify a person — name, address, SSN, phone, email
questions.explanation: PII is broader than people think. ZIP + birthdate + gender uniquely identifies most Americans.

questions.question: Crowdsourcing is:
options: Obtaining info, ideas, contributions from a large online group
questions.explanation: Wikipedia, Kickstarter, open source.

questions.question: Citizen science enabled by computing example:
options: Astronomy app letting volunteers classify galaxy images
questions.explanation: Galaxy Zoo, eBird, Foldit.

questions.question: Open-source software is:
options: Source code freely available to view, modify, distribute (under licenses)
questions.explanation: Open source ≠ no copyright. Licensed (MIT, GPL) but freely usable.

questions.question: A drawback of facial-recognition technology:
options: Privacy concerns + potential for surveillance, misuse, bias
questions.explanation: Evaluate BOTH benefits and harms.

questions.prompt: Match each algorithm to its worst-case time complexity.
questions.explanation: Linear scan = O(n). Binary halves = O(log n). Bubble compares every pair = O(n²). Hash maps directly to a bucket = O(1).

questions.question: Complete the code so it sums the values in nums.
questions.explanation: Initialize accumulator to 0, then add each value into it.
Returning true on the FIRST positive is wrong — we should only return true after confirming ALL are positive. Fix: return false on the first non-positive, then return true after the loop.

questions.title: Sort by speed (fastest first)
questions.description: Order these time complexities from fastest to slowest as n grows large.
questions.explanation: Constant beats logarithmic beats linear beats linearithmic beats quadratic. As n grows, gaps grow huge.

questions.question: What does triple(double(2)) return?
questions.code:
PROCEDURE double(n) { RETURN(n * 2) }
PROCEDURE triple(n) { RETURN(n * 3) }
questions.explanation: Inner first: double(2) = 4. Outer: triple(4) = 12. Procedures compose like math functions.

questions.title: Trace nested calls
questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.explanation: addOne(3) = 4. Then square(4) = 16. y is 16.

questions.title: Build absolute value
questions.description: Order these blocks to define a procedure absVal(n) that returns |n|, then call it on -7.
questions.explanation: Define before call. The IF returns -n for negatives; otherwise we fall through and return n unchanged.

questions.question: Complete the procedure to return true if n is divisible by 3.
questions.explanation: MOD gives the remainder. Remainder of 0 means evenly divisible — return true.

options: message is built but never RETURNed — caller gets nothing
questions.explanation: DISPLAY(greet(...)) needs greet to RETURN something. As written, the procedure builds a string then throws it away. Add RETURN(message).

options: When b > big the assignment uses the wrong variable — should be big ← b
questions.explanation: Copy-paste mistake: when b is the new biggest, big should become b, not c. Result: max3(1, 99, 0) returns 0 instead of 99.

questions.question: What does this code display?
questions.explanation: Multiplicative accumulator: 1*1*2*3*4 = 24. This is the factorial pattern.

questions.title: Trace the swap
questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.explanation: Classic swap. Without temp, a ← b would clobber the original a. Final: a=9, b=5.

questions.title: Trace the count-down
questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.explanation: i decrements before each display: 4, 2, 0. After 0 the condition i ≤ 0 holds → loop exits.

questions.title: Average of a list
questions.description: Order these blocks to compute and display the average of nums.
questions.explanation: Standard accumulate-then-divide. Watch out for empty lists — LENGTH would be 0 and you'd divide by zero.

questions.title: Reverse a list
questions.description: Order these blocks to display nums in reverse order.
questions.explanation: Inserting at position 1 each iteration pushes existing items back, building the reverse.

questions.question: Best abstraction for storing high temperatures every day for a year?
options: Either #2 or #3 works — pick by access pattern
questions.explanation: Both work. Flat list = easy yearly stats. 2D = easy per-month stats. Choose based on what you'll do most.

questions.title: Parallel-list lookup
questions.question: Final value of result?
questions.explanation: Loop finds 'Lee' at index 2, sets result = ages[2] = 17. The remaining iteration doesn't change anything.

questions.title: Walk a 2D grid
questions.question: What is displayed?
questions.explanation: Sum every cell of the 2D list: 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10.

questions.title: Display every cell of a 2D grid
questions.description: Order these blocks to display every cell of grid (row by row).
questions.explanation: Outer loop picks a row, inner loop walks that row's cells. Standard nested-loop walk.

questions.prompt: Match each scenario to the best AP-style data structure.
questions.explanation: Grid → 2D list. GPAs (numbers) → 1D number list. Two related fields → parallel lists. On/off → booleans.

questions.question: Which time complexity is reasonable to run on a list of 1 BILLION items?
questions.explanation: Linear is reasonable (1B steps completes in seconds-to-minutes). Quadratic = 10¹⁸ steps = years. Exponential and factorial are unimaginable past trivial sizes.

questions.question: Why might a heuristic be preferred over an exact algorithm?
options: When the exact answer would take too long, a 'good enough' answer fast is more useful
Heuristics are required by the AP exam
questions.explanation: GPS routing, recommender systems, scheduling — all use heuristics because exact optimum would take impractical time.

questions.title: Build linear search
questions.description: Order these blocks to return the index of target in aList, or 0 if not found.
questions.explanation: Walk one item at a time. On match, return its index. After the loop, return 0 as the 'not found' sentinel.

questions.prompt: Match each search/sort method to a key property.
questions.explanation: Linear works on anything. Binary requires sorted. Bubble compares all pairs → quadratic. Hash maps directly to a bucket → constant time average.

questions.title: Trace the loop boundary
questions.question: Final value of sum?
questions.explanation: REPEAT UNTIL i > 4 still executes the body when i = 4. So 1+2+3+4 = 10. A common off-by-one would use ≥ and miss the 4.

questions.title: Trace the broken exit
questions.question: What happens?
questions.explanation: i goes 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, ... never lands exactly on 5. Exit condition uses = instead of ≥, so the loop never terminates. Use ≥ for safety.

questions.title: Fix the all-positive check
questions.description: Order these blocks to correctly return true ONLY if every value in nums is positive.
questions.explanation: Common bug: returning true on the FIRST positive. Correct pattern: return false on first failure, then true after the loop confirms all passed.

options: Should be count ← count + 1; the expression's result is computed and discarded
questions.explanation: count + 1 evaluates a number and throws it away. Need an assignment: count ← count + 1.

options: Variable typo — DISPLAY(min) should be DISPLAY(max)
questions.explanation: Computes max correctly but then displays a never-defined variable 'min'. Probably copy-pasted from a min-finding routine.

questions.question: When does (NOT a) OR b evaluate to FALSE?
options: When a is true and b is false
When a is false and b is false
When a is true and b is true
When a is false and b is true
questions.explanation: (NOT a) OR b is false only when both halves are false. NOT a = false means a = true. b = false. So a=true, b=false.
Inside: true OR false = true. Then true AND false = false.
Left: NOT(false) = true. Right: NOT(true) = false. true AND false = false.
Left half: true. Right half: false. true OR false = true.

questions.question: Largest unsigned integer storable in 6 bits?
questions.explanation: 2⁶ = 64 distinct values, from 0 to 63. The MAX value is 63 = 111111.

questions.question: What uniquely identifies a device on a network?
questions.explanation: Each device gets a unique IP. Domain names map to IPs via DNS — but the IP is what the network actually uses.

questions.question: Why does packet switching make the Internet resilient?
options: If one route fails, packets take a different path automatically
Packets are smaller than original files
questions.explanation: Each packet is routed independently. A failed router doesn't kill the connection — packets reroute around it.

questions.question: What does TCP guarantee that UDP does not?
options: Reliable, in-order delivery (lost packets are resent)
questions.explanation: TCP confirms delivery and re-sends missing packets in order. UDP is fire-and-forget — faster, useful for video/games where occasional loss beats waiting.

questions.prompt: Match each Internet protocol/service to its job.
questions.explanation: DNS resolves names. HTTP is the web protocol. TCP makes packet streams reliable. IP routes packets. The classic stack.

questions.question: Doubling each side of an image (e.g., 100×100 → 200×200, same color depth) multiplies storage by:
questions.explanation: Doubling each dimension quadruples pixel count: 10,000 → 40,000 pixels. Storage scales with pixel count.

questions.question: What does 'lossless' compression guarantee?
options: Decompressed output is bit-for-bit identical to the original
questions.explanation: Lossless = exactly reversible (ZIP, PNG, FLAC). Lossy (JPEG, MP3) discards data and can't perfectly reconstruct.

questions.title: Trace bright-pixel count
questions.question: How many pixels exceed brightness 200?
questions.explanation: 220 and 240 exceed 200; 50, 180, 30 do not. Count = 2.

questions.question: Why should you NEVER reuse the same password across multiple sites?
options: If one site's database is breached, attackers try the same password on banking, email, etc. (credential stuffing)
questions.explanation: Credential stuffing is the #1 source of account takeovers today. A unique password per site contains the damage when (not if) one site leaks.

questions.question: What's the difference between encryption and a digital signature?
options: No real difference — both protect data
Encryption keeps a message secret; a signature proves WHO sent it and that it wasn't altered
questions.explanation: Different goals: encryption = confidentiality (keep contents secret); signatures = authenticity + integrity (prove sender, detect tampering). Both use keys but in different roles.

questions.question: A scam website using HTTPS is:
options: Still dangerous — HTTPS encrypts the channel but says nothing about the site's intent
questions.explanation: HTTPS protects you from someone snooping in the middle. It does not vouch for the site's honesty. Phishing sites use HTTPS too.

questions.question: An algorithm trained primarily on data from one demographic is most likely to:
options: Perform worse on under-represented demographics due to algorithmic bias
questions.explanation: Famous example: facial recognition systems trained mostly on light-skinned subjects had much higher error rates on dark-skinned subjects. Training data shapes outcomes.

questions.question: Which is a real benefit of open-source software?
options: You don't have to follow any license terms
Anyone can read the code, find security holes, and contribute fixes
It's always faster than proprietary software
questions.explanation: 'Many eyes' on the code helps catch bugs and security issues. Open-source IS licensed (MIT, GPL, Apache, etc.) — the source is just freely viewable and modifiable under those terms.

questions.question: Third-party tracking cookies that follow you across many sites are mainly used to:
options: Build a profile of your interests for targeted advertising
questions.explanation: Cross-site tracking links your activity into an ad profile. Modern Safari and Firefox block these cookies by default; Chrome is gradually doing the same.

questions.question: A delivery company's database stores: TruckID, weight (lbs), entry_time, exit_time. Which question CANNOT be answered using only this data?
options: How many trucks entered before noon?
What was the average truck weight today?
How many deliveries did each truck make?
How long was each truck on-site?
questions.explanation: The schema records weight and timestamps, but never the count of deliveries per truck. Trips ≠ deliveries — a truck could enter once and deliver to 20 customers, or enter five times with zero deliveries.

questions.question: A library tracks every book checkout: BookID, MemberID, checkout_date, return_date. Which question CANNOT be answered?
options: Which book is checked out most often?
Did the member enjoy reading the book?
Who has the most overdue returns?
What month has the highest checkout volume?
questions.explanation: Enjoyment isn't a field. The database knows what was borrowed and when — but not how the reader felt. Inferring 'enjoyment' from checkout duration would be a guess, not data.

questions.question: A hospital records each visit as: PatientID, arrival_time, department, diagnosis_code. Which question CAN be answered directly?
options: How long did each patient wait before being seen?
Which department had the most visits last month?
How much did each visit cost?
questions.explanation: Visits grouped by department and filtered by month is a straightforward filter+count. Wait times need a 'first-seen' timestamp, cost needs a billing field, satisfaction needs a survey — none are present.

questions.question: A school's attendance database stores: StudentID, date, period, status (present/absent/tardy). Which question requires data NOT in this database?
options: How many tardies did student #1042 get this semester?
Which period has the most absences across the school?
How many students were present on March 12?
questions.explanation: 'Status' tells you IF a student was absent, not WHY. Reasons (illness, family, skipping) would need to be a separate column. Classic exam trap — students assume rich data is there when only the labels are stored.

questions.question: An online store logs each order: OrderID, CustomerID, item, quantity, price, timestamp. Which question CANNOT be answered without additional data?
options: What was total revenue last week?
Which item sells the most units?
questions.explanation: No location field exists. Revenue (price × qty), top sellers (group by item), and repeat buyers (count by CustomerID) all use fields that ARE in the schema. Location would need a separate customer table or shipping records.

questions.question: A survey dataset accidentally contains 30 duplicate responses. If you compute the average satisfaction WITHOUT removing them, what happens?
options: The average is unaffected because duplicates have the same value
The average is biased toward the duplicated respondents' opinions
The average becomes mathematically impossible to compute
Duplicates always cancel each other out
questions.explanation: Counting the same person multiple times overweights their answer. If 30 duplicates were all very happy customers, average satisfaction will look higher than it really is. Cleaning means de-duplicating BEFORE aggregating.

questions.question: A spreadsheet of monthly rainfall has several blank cells where the sensor failed. A student computes the mean by SUM ÷ count_of_non_blank_cells. What's the risk?
options: No risk — skipping blanks is always correct
The result mis-represents reality if the missing months were systematically rainier or drier
Blank cells will be counted as zero automatically
The mean will always be too high
questions.explanation: If the sensor failed mostly during storms, the surviving data is biased toward dry months. The 'missingness' itself isn't random. Knowing WHY data is missing is often more important than the cleaning method.

questions.question: A column meant to hold ages contains values: 17, 18, '19', 20, 'twenty', 22. What's the MOST likely cause?
options: Inconsistent input — strings mixed with numbers, no validation at entry
Ages above 19 are stored as words
questions.explanation: Type errors come from forms without input validation. Cleaning means converting '19' → 19 and either fixing or dropping 'twenty'. Real analyses crash on rows like this until cleaning is done.

questions.question: A 'state' column contains: 'NY', 'New York', 'new york', 'N.Y.', 'NewYork'. A student counts rows where state = 'NY' to find New York entries. What goes wrong?
options: Nothing — the database matches all five automatically
Only the exact match 'NY' is counted; the other four variants are missed
questions.explanation: String matches are literal. Cleaning means standardizing formats — pick one representation ('NY') and convert all variants to it before counting.

questions.question: A retail dataset has a row where 'price' = 9999999. The next-highest price is $300. Which is the BEST first response?
options: Delete the row immediately and don't tell anyone
Include it — all data is sacred
Investigate whether it's a placeholder, typo, or legitimate outlier before deciding
Replace it with the average price
questions.explanation: 9999999 is often a placeholder for 'unknown'. Or a typo (9.99 → 9999999). Or rarely a real luxury item. Cleaning decisions need context, not reflexes. Document whatever you decide.

questions.question: Given a table of customer orders, you want a list of customers who spent more than $500 TOTAL. Which sequence of operations is correct?
options: Sort by name, then filter by total
Group by customer → sum spending → filter where sum > 500
Filter where price > 500 first, then group
Just look at the highest single order
questions.explanation: You need TOTAL spending per customer, so aggregate first (sum per customer), THEN apply the threshold filter. Filtering individual rows by price > 500 misses customers with many smaller orders that add up.

questions.question: A dataset has 50,000 song-play records. To answer 'which song is most popular?', what transformation is needed?
options: Group by SongID and count plays; sort descending; take the top row
Compute the average of all play counts
Sort the raw records alphabetically by song name
questions.explanation: Raw records don't tell you popularity directly — you have to aggregate. Group → count → sort → top is the standard pipeline for 'what's the most X' questions.

questions.question: A weather dataset has daily high temperatures for 10 years. To produce 'average high temperature by month', you need to:
options: Average every single value in the dataset
Group rows by their MONTH (ignoring year), then average within each group
Pick one year and report its monthly averages
questions.explanation: 'By month' means the group key is the month. All 10 Januarys collapse into one bucket; same for February, etc. The transformation reduces ~3,650 rows to 12.

questions.question: Why do filtering and transforming data turn DATA into INFORMATION?
options: Aggregating raw rows reveals patterns and answers questions a human cares about
Information is whatever data is left over after filtering
questions.explanation: Raw data ('30 million temperature readings') is hard to act on. Information ('July is warmer than January in NYC') is what humans can decide with. The transformation IS the act of creating meaning.

questions.question: A student wants to know how many female students scored above 90 on the final. The table has columns: StudentID, name, gender, score. Best plan?
options: Sort by score, then count manually
Filter gender = 'F' AND score > 90, then count rows
Group by gender then average the scores
questions.explanation: Two-condition filter, then count. A single combined filter is simpler than filtering twice. This is the pattern behind every 'how many X with property Y' database question on the exam.

questions.question: What is metadata?
options: A backup copy of the data
Data ABOUT data — describing its origin, format, size, time created, etc.
The most important data in a dataset
questions.explanation: Metadata describes the data without being the data itself. A photo's pixels are the data; the camera model, GPS, and timestamp embedded in the file are metadata.

questions.question: When you take a photo on your phone, what metadata is typically stored INSIDE the image file?
options: Camera model, exposure settings, timestamp, and often GPS location
The names of every person in the photo
questions.explanation: EXIF metadata travels with the photo. That's why sharing an unmodified photo can reveal where and when you were — a real privacy concern. Many social platforms strip EXIF on upload, but not all.

questions.question: Why can email metadata (sender, recipient, time, subject) sometimes reveal MORE than the email's content?
options: Metadata is always written in all caps
Patterns of who-talks-to-whom-when expose relationships, schedules, and networks without needing the message text
Metadata is plain text and content is encrypted
Metadata files are larger than message files
questions.explanation: You don't need to read messages to learn that someone calls their doctor weekly at 9 PM, or that two executives email moments before a stock trade. This is why metadata collection is a major privacy debate.

questions.question: Which of these is metadata for a music file (.mp3), NOT the data itself?
options: The waveform samples that produce sound
Artist name, album, track length, bitrate, year released
The volume the song plays at
The speakers the user listens with
questions.explanation: Audio samples = the data (what you hear). ID3 tags = metadata (artist, album, length). Music apps sort and search using metadata; you couldn't browse by 'artist' without it.

questions.question: Why do search engines, recommendation systems, and digital libraries depend on metadata?
options: Metadata is always more accurate than the content
Searching, filtering, and grouping over millions of files is fast on small tagged fields but slow on full content
Metadata is required by federal law
Without metadata, files can't be opened
questions.explanation: Indexing 'artist = Beyoncé' across a million songs is fast. Re-analyzing audio waveforms of every song would be enormously slower. Good metadata makes large-scale search practical.

questions.question: A startup builds a music recommendation algorithm using only data from its current users — mostly college students aged 18–22. What's the risk when the app launches to a broader audience?
options: The algorithm will work perfectly for everyone
Recommendations will be biased toward what 18–22-year-olds like, performing poorly for older users whose tastes weren't represented
Older users will get college-student discounts automatically
questions.explanation: If the training data only represents one demographic, the model learns THAT demographic's patterns. This is the canonical 'unrepresentative training data' bias the AP exam loves to ask about.

questions.question: A city posts a public survey link on its website asking 'How satisfied are you with city services?'. Why might the results be biased?
options: People without reliable internet — often older, lower-income, or rural — are systematically underrepresented
questions.explanation: The digital divide creates sampling bias. Whoever isn't online can't be heard. Cities that rely only on online surveys miss the residents most likely to struggle with services in the first place.

questions.question: A hiring algorithm is trained on 10 years of past hiring decisions at a company. The company historically hired few women into engineering roles. What does the algorithm likely learn?
options: It learns to be perfectly fair because data is neutral
It learns to replicate the historical pattern, ranking women lower even if their qualifications are equal
It learns nothing because hiring data is too small
questions.explanation: Models learn from whatever patterns the data shows — including discriminatory ones. 'Garbage in, garbage out' applies to bias too. Amazon famously shelved a recruiting tool for exactly this reason.

questions.question: A medical study collects symptom data only from patients who visit a clinic. Why is the resulting dataset NOT representative of the general population's health?
options: People who can't afford care, live far from clinics, or distrust doctors are missing — yet they're part of 'the population'
questions.explanation: This is selection bias. The dataset only captures people who chose AND were able to seek care. Conclusions drawn ('symptom X is rare') may be wildly wrong for the larger uncared-for population.

questions.question: What's the SAFEST conclusion a researcher can draw when key demographic groups are MISSING from a dataset?
options: Whatever the data shows applies to everyone
Conclusions are valid only for the groups actually represented; extrapolating beyond them is unsupported
Missing groups should be assumed to behave the same as represented groups
Missing data should be filled in with random values
questions.explanation: You can only generalize to whoever was actually sampled. Pretending missing groups are 'just like' the included ones is the assumption that creates real-world harm — from medical dosing to facial recognition failures.

questions.question: What is a computer simulation?
options: A photograph of a real-world event
A program that models a real-world or hypothetical system using simplifying rules and assumptions
An exact perfect copy of reality
questions.explanation: Simulations represent systems abstractly — flight simulators, disease-spread models, climate models, traffic models. They make assumptions because reality is too detailed to copy exactly.

questions.question: Why might scientists use a simulation INSTEAD of a real-world experiment?
options: Simulations are always more accurate than experiments
Real experiments may be too dangerous, expensive, slow, or impossible to repeat at scale
questions.explanation: You can't crash 1,000 cars to test bumpers, or speed up climate, or rerun a pandemic. Simulations let you explore 'what-ifs' safely and cheaply. The trade-off is that they're only as good as the model.

questions.question: A traffic simulation assumes every driver follows the speed limit exactly. The simulation predicts no accidents. Why is this prediction unreliable?
options: The simplifying assumption (perfect drivers) doesn't match reality, so any conclusion built on it is suspect
questions.explanation: Every simulation makes simplifying assumptions. The validity of its predictions depends on whether those assumptions hold. 'Perfect drivers' obviously doesn't — so 'no accidents' tells you about the model, not the road.

questions.question: Which of these is a key LIMITATION of every simulation?
options: A simulation can only be as accurate as the model it's built from — missing factors lead to wrong predictions
Simulations must always run faster than real time
questions.explanation: Models omit detail by design. A weather simulation that ignores cloud cover will mispredict temperature. AP exam questions love asking 'what couldn't this simulation tell us?' — the answer is usually whatever the model leaves out.

questions.question: Why do many simulations (disease spread, weather, gambling) use RANDOM number generators?
options: To make results harder to verify
Real-world systems involve chance; running the simulation many times with random inputs explores the range of possible outcomes
Random numbers are required by AP exam rules
questions.explanation: A single simulation run with fixed inputs gives one answer. Repeated runs with random variation (Monte Carlo methods) reveal the distribution — 'in 80% of runs, the disease dies out by week 8' is more useful than one trajectory.

questions._section: Mixed practice — 5 per topic across all 11 topics

questions.title: Trace adjust(4, 9)
questions.question: What value is displayed?
questions.explanation: 4 > 9 is false, so the ELSE branch runs: y - x = 9 - 4 = 5.

questions.title: Build: average of a list
questions.description: Order these blocks to define a procedure that returns the average of the values in nums.
questions.explanation: Initialize an accumulator, sum every element, then return sum ÷ count. The RETURN must sit OUTSIDE the loop or the procedure exits after one element.

questions.question: Complete the procedure so it returns triple its input.
questions.explanation: The parameter declares the input variable name (x). The body should multiply that VARIABLE by 3 — that way triple(5) gives 15, but triple(7) also works.

questions.question: A team has the same email-validation logic copy-pasted in 12 places. They refactor into one procedure isValidEmail(s). What is the BIGGEST benefit?
options: If the validation rule changes, fixing it once updates all 12 call sites
The procedure takes less memory than 12 copies
It removes the need for testing
questions.explanation: Procedural abstraction's win is maintainability, not speed. Twelve copies meant twelve places to fix when (not if) the rule changes.

questions.question: What's the key difference between RETURN(x) and DISPLAY(x) inside a procedure?
options: RETURN sends x back to the caller as a usable value; DISPLAY only shows x on screen and doesn't help the caller compute anything
DISPLAY ends the procedure; RETURN does not
questions.explanation: DISPLAY is a side effect — useful for the user, useless to other code. RETURN feeds the value back so y ← square(5) works. Confusing them is a common exam trap.

questions.title: Count positives
questions.question: What value is displayed?
questions.explanation: Three positives in the list: 4, 5, 3. Zero is not positive.

questions.title: Find the maximum
questions.description: Order these blocks so the program ends with the largest value in nums stored in best.
questions.explanation: Seed the candidate with the first element, then compare each value. Initializing best to 0 would break for all-negative lists.

questions.question: Complete the code so it counts how many times target appears in nums.
questions.explanation: Start the counter at 0. Increment only when the current element equals the target.

questions.question: When should you prefer REPEAT N TIMES over REPEAT UNTIL?
options: When you know in advance EXACTLY how many iterations you need
Only when N is greater than 100
When the loop body modifies a list
questions.explanation: REPEAT N TIMES = known count. REPEAT UNTIL (condition) = unknown count, stop when something becomes true. Picking the wrong one is a common AP exam trap.

questions.question: AP pseudocode uses 1-based list indexing. To visit EVERY element of a list of length n with an indexed loop, the index should run from:
questions.explanation: AP pseudocode: first element is list[1], last is list[n]. Many real languages use 0 to n-1, but the AP exam uses 1 to n.

questions.title: Walk a 2D list
questions.question: What value is displayed?
questions.explanation: row[2] is the SECOND element of each row in AP pseudocode (1-indexed). Second column: 2 + 5 + 8 = 15.

questions.prompt: Match each scenario to the BEST data abstraction.
questions.explanation: Grid → 2D list. Many similar items → 1D list. Heterogeneous fields → object. Repeated yes/no → list of booleans.

questions.title: Build a filter
questions.description: Order these blocks so adults ends up containing every value from ages that is at least 18.
questions.explanation: Filter pattern: start with an empty list, walk the input, append only items that pass the test.

questions.question: Complete the code so highCount ends up as the number of scores at or above 90.
questions.explanation: Filter with ≥, then increment a counter (not the score itself) by 1 per match.

questions.question: A program tracks students. You can store data as parallel lists (names[i], grades[i], gpas[i]) OR as a single list of records. Why are parallel lists RISKIER?
options: Parallel lists run slower than records
If one list is sorted or filtered without the others, the indexing breaks and a student gets wrong attributes
questions.explanation: Parallel lists rely on every list staying in lock-step. One careless sort and student #5's name lines up with someone else's GPA. Records bind fields together so they can't drift apart.

questions.title: Binary search steps
questions.description: Order the steps performed by binary search when looking up a target in a SORTED list.
items: Look at the middle element of the current range
If target is smaller, repeat on the left half; otherwise repeat on the right half
If the range becomes empty, report not found
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search range each step. That's why it's O(log n) — doubling n only adds one comparison.

questions.prompt: Match each problem to the most appropriate strategy.
left: Find a name in a sorted phone book
Largest value in an unsorted list
Does any program halt on any input?
questions.explanation: Sorted lookup → binary search. Combinatorial route → heuristic (TSP is intractable at scale). Unsorted scan → linear. Halting problem → undecidable.

questions.question: An algorithm runs in 2^n steps. If n=10 takes 1 second, roughly how long does n=30 take?
questions.explanation: Doubling 20 more times → 2^20 ≈ 1,000,000× slower. Exponential growth is why brute-force password cracking and worst-case traveling-salesman blow up so fast.

questions.question: A delivery company has 500 stops to plan TODAY. Finding the absolute shortest route would take a supercomputer years. Why is a heuristic acceptable?
options: A near-optimal route delivered today is far more valuable than the perfect route delivered next year
Heuristics use less memory and that's the bottleneck
questions.explanation: Real business value comes from 'good enough, on time'. GPS routing, search ranking, ad targeting — all intentionally approximate because exact answers are infeasible at scale.

questions.question: Algorithm A is O(n), Algorithm B is O(n²). Both solve the same problem. For very large n, which statement is TRUE?
options: B is faster because it does more work
A scales much better; doubling n roughly doubles A's time but quadruples B's time
They run at the same speed for any input
B is preferred because it's more thorough
questions.explanation: O(n) is linear: 2× input → 2× time. O(n²) is quadratic: 2× input → 4× time. For n = 1,000,000, B is roughly 1,000,000× slower than A.

options: Operator precedence: '/ LENGTH - 1' divides first, then subtracts 1 — missing parentheses give the wrong average
questions.explanation: Should be RETURN(total / LENGTH(nums)). The '- 1' is leftover from someone confusing index ranges with the divisor. Classic copy-paste-then-edit slip.
RETURN(false) is INSIDE the loop, so the function exits after checking only the first element. Move it OUTSIDE the loop — it should run only if no negatives were found.

questions.question: A program works on [3, 5, 8] and [10, 12, 14] but produces wrong output on the empty list []. What's the BEST next step?
options: Mark the program done — empty lists are unusual
Trace the code by hand with [] and add a guard or fix the logic where it breaks
questions.explanation: Empty lists, single-element lists, and 'null' values are classic edge cases the exam expects you to recognize. Pinpoint where the failure happens, fix that specific case.

options: Logic inverted: returns TRUE when a failing score is found; the procedure name promises the opposite
questions.explanation: Either flip the comparison and swap the return values, or rename the procedure. As written, allPass([100, 95, 90]) would return false. Names lying to readers is one of the worst bug categories.

questions.question: You're testing a function that grades scores 0–100 into letter grades. Which set of test cases is BEST?
options: One input from the middle of each range
Boundary values like 0, 59, 60, 69, 70, 79, 80, 89, 90, 100 — plus out-of-range values like -5 and 105
Only valid grades from A and B
questions.explanation: Bugs hide at boundaries (off-by-one between B and C, etc.) and on out-of-range input. Random sampling rarely hits them; deliberate boundary testing does.
Inside parens: NOT(false) = true, then true OR true = true. Then true AND false = false.
Innermost: NOT(false) = true. Then true AND true = true. Outer NOT(true) = false.
Left: false OR false = false. Right: NOT(false) = true. false OR true = true.

questions.question: Which expression is EQUIVALENT to NOT(A AND B)?
questions.explanation: De Morgan's Law: NOT(A AND B) = NOT(A) OR NOT(B). 'Not both' = 'at least one isn't'. Frequently tested on the AP exam in disguise.

questions.question: The condition (x > 10) AND (x > 5) can be simplified to:
questions.explanation: If x > 10, then x > 5 is automatic. The stricter condition wins — any x that passes both must already exceed 10.

questions.question: How many distinct values can be represented with 10 bits?
questions.explanation: n bits → 2^n combinations. 2^10 = 1024. Each added bit DOUBLES the range — and the overflow risk.

questions.question: An 8-bit counter holds the value 255. The program adds 1. What is the MOST LIKELY result?
options: Overflow — the counter wraps to 0 or signals an error, because 8 bits max out at 255
questions.explanation: 8 bits → max unsigned value 255 (11111111). +1 needs a 9th bit. Either the result wraps to 0 or an overflow error is reported, depending on the language.

questions.prompt: Match each Internet term to its role.
right: A device that forwards packets between networks
A small chunk of a larger message, sent independently
A human-readable label (example.com) for an IP
A numeric address identifying a device on the network
questions.explanation: IP = numeric address. Domain = name. Packet = chunk. Router = forwarder. The classic four-piece quiz the AP exam loves.

questions.question: What does HTTPS provide that HTTP does NOT?
options: Encryption of data in transit AND verification that the server owns the claimed domain
questions.explanation: HTTPS = HTTP + TLS. TLS encrypts so eavesdroppers can't read your traffic, and the certificate proves the server is who it claims to be.

questions.question: The Internet has been migrating from IPv4 (32-bit addresses) to IPv6 (128-bit addresses). What is the main reason?
options: IPv4 only allows about 4 billion addresses — not enough for every device on Earth
IPv6 is more secure than IPv4
questions.explanation: 2^32 ≈ 4 billion addresses ran out as phones, cars, sensors, and appliances joined the Internet. 2^128 addresses (IPv6) — a vastly larger space — solves that.

questions.question: Internet protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS) are 'open standards'. Why is that important?
options: Anyone can read and implement them, which is why devices from different vendors interoperate
Open standards are required by law
Open means free of charge only after paying a license
Open standards run faster than closed ones
questions.explanation: Open standards = published, royalty-free, vendor-neutral. The reason your iPhone can talk to a Windows server through a Cisco router. The Internet's scale depends on this.

questions.question: Bandwidth and latency are different measures. Which statement is correct?
options: Bandwidth and latency mean the same thing
Bandwidth = how much data per second; latency = how long a single message takes to arrive
questions.explanation: A fat pipe with long delay (satellite) has high bandwidth but high latency. A thin pipe with short delay (local Ethernet) is the reverse. Both matter — video calls need low latency; large downloads need high bandwidth.

questions.question: An image uses 24 bits per pixel (8 each for R, G, B). How many distinct colors can a single pixel represent?
questions.explanation: Each channel: 2^8 = 256 levels. Combined: 256 × 256 × 256 ≈ 16.7 million. Why 'True Color' is enough — the human eye can't distinguish more.

questions.question: Sound is digitized by SAMPLING the wave many times per second. What's the consequence of a LOWER sampling rate?
options: Smaller file size, but the recording loses high-frequency detail and sounds 'muffled' or distorted
questions.explanation: Fewer samples per second → coarser digital approximation of the wave. CDs sample at 44,100 Hz; phone audio is often 8,000 Hz — fine for voice, terrible for music.

questions.question: Which scenario is BEST suited to LOSSY compression?
options: Streaming a movie — small differences in pixels and audio go unnoticed but bandwidth is saved enormously
questions.explanation: Lossy compression discards detail humans don't perceive. Perfect for movies, music, JPEGs. Bad for contracts, code, or medical images where every byte matters.

questions.question: What's the fundamental difference between analog and digital signals?
options: Analog signals are continuous over time and value; digital signals are discrete, represented as samples or bits
questions.explanation: Vinyl (analog) = continuous groove. CD (digital) = numbered samples. Digital can be copied perfectly and processed by computers; analog degrades each generation.

questions.question: An uncompressed image is 100 × 100 pixels at 24 bits per pixel. About how many bytes is the raw image?
questions.explanation: 100 × 100 = 10,000 pixels. 24 bits × 10,000 = 240,000 bits. 240,000 ÷ 8 = 30,000 bytes (~30 KB). This is why compression matters — uncompressed photos balloon fast.

questions.question: What's the key difference between symmetric and asymmetric (public-key) encryption?
options: Symmetric is more secure than asymmetric
Symmetric uses ONE shared key; asymmetric uses a PAIR of keys (public to encrypt, private to decrypt)
Asymmetric only works for short messages
questions.explanation: Symmetric (AES) is fast but requires both parties to share a secret. Asymmetric (RSA) lets strangers communicate securely — publish the public key, keep the private one. HTTPS uses both.

questions.question: In public-key encryption, which key is used to ENCRYPT a message you want only Alice to read?
questions.explanation: Encrypt with Alice's PUBLIC key (anyone has it). Only her matching PRIVATE key can decrypt. That's the asymmetry — it travels one direction safely.

questions.question: A 'phishing' attack typically works by:
options: Tricking the user into revealing credentials via a fake login page or urgent-looking message
Exploiting a software bug in the browser
questions.explanation: Phishing exploits the HUMAN, not the software. The fake 'Your account is locked, click here' email is the classic. No firewall stops a user from typing their password into the wrong site.

questions.question: When you submit a form over HTTPS, who is BLOCKED from reading your input?
options: Intermediate parties (Wi-Fi snoopers, ISPs, routers) — but the website's server still sees the plain data on arrival
Nobody — HTTPS is just for show
The browser, but not the server
questions.explanation: HTTPS encrypts data in transit, so people between you and the server can't read it. But the server decrypts on arrival — it has to, to use the data. HTTPS ≠ 'nobody sees this'.

questions.question: Which password is the STRONGEST against modern cracking attacks?
questions.explanation: Length beats complexity. A four-word random passphrase has more entropy than 'P@ssw0rd!' (which is in every cracker's dictionary). Humans remember length better than randomness.

questions.question: Why does adding ALT TEXT to every image on a website matter?
options: Alt text is required by AP CSP rules
Screen readers read alt text aloud, making the site usable for blind and low-vision users — and it also helps when images fail to load
Alt text makes pages load faster
It's only useful for search engine optimization
questions.explanation: Accessibility is a CED-tested topic. Alt text is the canonical example: a single thoughtful design choice that makes the web usable for millions who'd otherwise be locked out.

questions.question: A school district adopts an AI tool that automatically grades student essays. Which BEST captures the dual nature of computing innovations?
options: It will only have beneficial effects
It saves teachers time, but may grade essays from non-native English speakers unfairly or miss creative writing styles outside its training data
It will only have harmful effects
It has no effects worth considering
questions.explanation: AP CSP explicitly tests recognizing BOTH benefits and harms of computing. Most innovations are dual-edged — the right answer almost always acknowledges trade-offs, not absolutes.

questions.question: Which scenario is MOST clearly a copyright violation?
options: Quoting a paragraph from a book in a school essay with citation
Uploading a full movie to YouTube without the studio's permission
Writing original code that solves the same problem as a popular open-source library
questions.explanation: Full copyrighted work uploaded without permission = clear violation. Citing a quote is fair use. Independently writing similar code isn't copying. Public domain has no protection to violate.

questions.question: Self-checkout kiosks at grocery stores illustrate which TWO simultaneous effects of computing innovations?
options: Lower checkout costs for the store AND reduced demand for cashier jobs
questions.explanation: The AP exam frames innovations as having combined effects — economic, social, employment. Self-checkout is the textbook example: efficiency for stores, job displacement for workers.

questions.question: Algorithms now influence decisions in lending, hiring, parole, and insurance. What is the BIGGEST societal concern?
options: When training data reflects past human bias, the algorithm can perpetuate or amplify that bias — at scale, behind a veneer of 'neutral math'
questions.explanation: The harm isn't that computers decide — it's that biased decisions get made faster, with less accountability, and assumed objective. AP CSP tests this concern directly under 'Bias in computing'.
